articles.title: Tech Firm Veeva Relocates to 62K-SF Office at Vornado’s Penn 2
articles.narrative: Veeva Systems, a data firm specializing in software and AI solutions for the life sciences sector, has signed a 10-year lease for 62,000 square feet at Vornado Realty Trust's Penn 2 in New York City. The company is relocating its offices from a nearby location at 1375 Broadway. The asking rent for the space was $110 per square foot.

data_points.address: 2 Pennsylvania Plaza, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Located near Pennsylvania Station.
data_points.project_notes: Lease term is 10 years.

articles.title: In Business — and in Life — Persistence Is a Major Competitive Advantage
articles.narrative: This opinion piece argues that persistence is a crucial competitive advantage in business and life, particularly in commercial real estate. The author uses the "Stonecutter's Creed" analogy to illustrate that success often appears sudden to observers but is, in fact, the cumulative result of countless small, consistent efforts over time. The central argument is that many people abandon sound processes too soon, mistaking a lack of visible progress for a lack of actual progress. The author emphasizes that identifying the right activities and committing to a sound process, followed by consistent execution, is key to achieving extraordinary results.

articles.title: AI Startup Phia Signs 9K-SF Lease at Zar Property’s 37 East 18th Street
articles.narrative: AI startup Phia, co-founded by Phoebe Gates and Sophia Kianni, has signed a lease for 8,900 square feet on the penthouse level of 37 East 18th Street in Manhattan. The deal represents a relocation for the company, which develops an AI-powered shopping assistant, and includes access to a portion of the building's roof deck. The landlord, Zar Property NY, was represented in-house, while JLL brokered the deal for the tenant.

data_points.address: 37 East 18th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Penthouse level space, includes a portion of the building's roof deck.
data_points.project_notes: Building previously acquired by Zar Property NY in September 2024 for $27 million.

articles.market_intelligence: Tech firm leasing is active in the Flatiron District area, with Clay signing a 163,095 square foot lease at 11 Madison Avenue in March. Hudson Square is also seeing significant tech tenant activity, including PayPal's 261,000 square foot deal at 345 Hudson Street and Anthropic in talks for 465,630 square feet at 330 Hudson Street.

articles.title: Developer Moses Hershko, From California to South Florida: 5 Questions
articles.narrative: Moses Hershko Development, a luxury homebuilder known for its work in California, is expanding its operations to South Florida with plans for high-end condos and mansions. The firm is developing a 28-unit boutique condominium project in Miami Beach, with units priced between $20 million and $200 million. Additionally, the company is in the design phase for a single-family mansion in Indian Creek, where land costs alone can reach $70 million to $80 million. The firm recently launched MHD Capital and raised $250 million from investors to support its real estate ventures.

data_points.notable_features: Boutique condo project with units priced from $20 million to $200 million.
data_points.project_notes: The firm is also working on a single-family mansion in Indian Creek, where land costs are estimated at $70 million to $80 million.

articles.title: Lenny’s Repair Takes 14K SF at Red Hook’s 590 Smith Street
articles.narrative: Lenny's Repair, a phone wholesaler, has signed a five-and-a-half-year lease for 14,000 square feet of flex warehouse and office space at 590 Smith Street in Red Hook, Brooklyn. The deal represents a consolidation for the repair business, which will occupy an upper floor of the three-story building. The landlord, Yeshiva of Kasho, also recently leased 7,000 square feet to medical wholesaler CW Health, reflecting Red Hook's appeal to industrial and flex tenants seeking more affordable options compared to redeveloping areas like Gowanus.

data_points.notable_features: Industrial and office building encompassing 582-590 Smith Street, located a block east of Red Hook Park.
data_points.project_notes: The landlord previously acquired the 30,000-square-foot property for $7.51 million in 2017.

articles.title: InterFace Panel: Affordable Housing Deal Flow Gains Momentum as Brokers Turn Bullish
articles.narrative: The affordable housing transaction market in the Southeast is experiencing a significant resurgence after a period of slower activity, driven by strong renter demand, persistent housing shortages, and improving capital market conditions. Despite ongoing challenges such as elevated borrowing costs and interest rate volatility, investors are showing renewed urgency to deploy capital, leading to a notable increase in transaction volume. Brokers and developers are adapting to a "new normal" where traditional refinancing options are less available, pushing owners to sell legacy assets, particularly older acquisition-rehab properties that require substantial capital investment.

people.name: Doug Childers
people.title: Senior Managing Director and Affordable Housing Leader

people.name: Cory Sams
people.title: Executive Managing Director and Co-Head of Affordable Housing

articles.title: Dallas Stars Plan New Arena, Mixed-Use Entertainment District in Plano
articles.narrative: The Dallas Stars are planning a new hockey arena and mixed-use entertainment district in Plano, Texas, at The Shops at Willow Bend. The project, estimated at $1 billion, will include sports-themed retail, restaurants, entertainment, and public spaces. The City of Plano is expected to contribute approximately $700 million towards the financing and will own the arena and site, entering into a 30-year lease with the team's parent company. This move follows a similar announcement by the Dallas Mavericks, as both teams' leases at American Airlines Center expire in 2031.

data_points.notable_features: The project includes a new hockey arena, sports-themed retail, restaurants, entertainment uses, and public gathering spaces.
data_points.project_notes: The City of Plano is expected to contribute approximately 70% of the financing and will own the arena and site, entering into a 30-year lease with the team's parent company. The Shops at Willow Bend is a 1.4 million-square-foot shopping, dining, and entertainment destination currently undergoing redevelopment.

articles.title: SurePoint Breaks Ground on 861-Unit Self-Storage Facility in The Woodlands, Texas
articles.narrative: SurePoint Development has broken ground on an 861-unit self-storage facility in The Woodlands, Texas. The project will feature 90,000 net rentable square feet of climate-controlled space and is expected to be completed in spring 2027. This marks SurePoint's tenth facility in the Houston metropolitan area.

data_points.notable_features: Climate-controlled space.
data_points.project_notes: This is SurePoint's 10th facility in the Houston area.

articles.title: Premier PV Signs 265,400 SF Industrial Lease in Southwest Dallas
articles.narrative: Premier PV, a solar photovoltaic products and services provider, has signed a 265,400-square-foot industrial lease in southwest Dallas. The building, located at 4715 Mountain Creek Parkway, was constructed in 2004 and recently renovated. The landlord, CanTex Capital, was represented by Holt Lunsford Commercial, while Site Selection Group represented the tenant.

data_points.notable_features: Features 34.5-foot clear heights and six acres for future expansion or outdoor storage.
data_points.project_notes: Building was renovated in 2025.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap Brokers Sale of Retail Strip Center in Balch Springs, Texas
articles.narrative: Marcus & Millichap has brokered the sale of Imperial Square, a retail strip center located in Balch Springs, Texas. The property, built in 2017, features seven suites occupied by service-oriented retailers. The buyer and seller were not disclosed in the transaction.

data_points.address: 3534 Hickory Tree Rd, Balch Springs, TX
data_points.notable_features: Comprises seven suites leased to a mix of service-oriented retailers.

articles.title: The great mall divide: Coresight report finds that top-tier malls thrive while lower-tier properties struggle
articles.narrative: A new report from Coresight Research highlights a significant bifurcation in the U.S. enclosed shopping mall sector, with top-tier properties thriving while lower-tier assets continue to struggle. The study, titled "The American Mall Renaissance: A Bifurcated Sector with Top-Tier Assets Leading the Way," indicates that metrics such as foot traffic, net operating income, rents, and occupancy are moving in opposite directions for these two categories of malls. Top-tier malls, often featuring luxury retailers in affluent areas, have nearly recovered to pre-pandemic foot traffic levels and boast high occupancy, while lower-tier malls lag significantly. Mall owners are increasingly adapting by replacing traditional department store vacancies with entertainment, fitness, and healthcare concepts to enhance the consumer experience and generate higher rents, a strategy that also benefits many mid-tier malls appealing to younger demographics. However, lower-tier malls face a challenging cycle of declining traffic and tenant departures, with redevelopment into alternative uses like distribution centers or mixed-use projects often being their most viable path forward.

people.name: John Mercer
people.title: Head of global research and managing director of data-driven research

articles.title: Colliers multifamily report: “Stabilizing” the best way to describe national multifamily market
articles.narrative: The U.S. multifamily sector is entering a phase of gradual stabilization, according to Colliers' Q1 2026 report. This rebalancing is driven by a slowdown in new construction and sustained renter demand, largely due to high single-family home costs and elevated mortgage rates. National apartment occupancy held steady at 95.1%, with effective monthly rents averaging $1,934. While Sun Belt markets still face high supply, Midwest and select coastal markets are seeing stronger occupancy due to more restrained construction activity. Investors are selectively targeting markets with easing supply pressures and strong long-term fundamentals, with cap rates stabilizing in the mid-5% range.
articles.market_intelligence: National apartment occupancy stood at 95.1% in Q1 2026, almost unchanged from the previous quarter and slightly below 95.2% a year earlier. Effective monthly rents averaged $1,934 nationally, up from $1,894 a year ago. National absorption reached over 85,000 units in Q1, outpacing the roughly 64,500 new units delivered. 501,117 units were under construction nationally at the end of Q1 2026, down from over 573,000 units a year earlier. Chicago posted a 96.3% occupancy rate in Q1, with effective rents increasing 3.5% year-over-year to $2,240 per month. Milwaukee also recorded a 96.3% occupancy rate and 2.1% annual rent growth. Cap rates have largely stabilized in the mid-5% range nationally.

articles.title: Commercial construction company TEXBUILT launches in Texas
articles.narrative: TEXBUILT has officially launched as a new commercial construction company focused on the Texas market. As an employee-owned entity under BLDR Holdings, TEXBUILT aims to leverage local leadership and a dedicated Texas-based team to deliver commercial construction services across various sectors. The company's leadership emphasizes a commitment to the region and building on existing relationships established through its affiliate, EMJ Construction, to serve the rapidly growing Texas market.

articles.title: Kraus-Anderson completes $100 million renovation, addition at New Town casino
articles.narrative: Kraus-Anderson has completed a $100 million renovation and addition project at 4 Bears Casino & Lodge in New Town, North Dakota. The extensive project involved demolishing the previous hotel, remodeling the existing casino, and constructing a new seven-story hotel tower that added 90 guest rooms, bringing the total to 264 units. The upgrades also included expanding the gaming area, converting a restaurant into a sports bar, and adding a ballroom, meeting rooms, a fine dining steakhouse, and a new spa.

data_points.notable_features: Features a new 7-story hotel tower, expanded gaming area, sports bar, ballroom, fine dining steakhouse, and Sakakawea Spa. Located on the western shore of Lake Sakakawea, a popular recreational area.
data_points.project_notes: Construction of the new hotel tower began in June 2023. The project included the demolition of a former 2-story hotel.

articles.title: CRESCO Real Estate lands industrial portfolio in Valley View
articles.narrative: Cushman & Wakefield | CRESCO Real Estate has been retained to market the PAG Portfolio, an eight-building industrial flex portfolio totaling 400,000 square feet across three campuses in Valley View, Ohio. The properties, located along Rockside Road, represent a significant industrial flex opportunity in a submarket where CRESCO has a strong presence. The brokerage plans to reposition some existing office space within the buildings to better align with current industrial flex tenant demand.

data_points.notable_features: Portfolio consists of eight industrial flex buildings across three campuses along Rockside Road.
data_points.project_notes: The strategy includes repositioning portions of existing single-story office space to add an industrial flex component.

articles.title: Commercial tenant drops Cushman suit over data breach
articles.narrative: A commercial tenant has voluntarily dismissed a potential class-action lawsuit against Cushman & Wakefield, less than a month after it was filed. The lawsuit alleged that the brokerage firm failed to protect clients' personal information, which was compromised in a recent cyberattack. While the reason for the dismissal was not disclosed, it was filed without prejudice, allowing for the possibility of future re-filing.

articles.title: White House attempts to bring down construction costs
articles.narrative: The White House has implemented temporary tariff adjustments on imported steel, aluminum, and copper, reducing levies on construction-related equipment from 25 percent to 15 percent until the end of next year. This measure aims to alleviate the burden of soaring material costs that have been impacting homebuilders, driven by geopolitical events and supply chain disruptions. The administration also introduced a future incentive for imports with high domestic content. However, it remains uncertain if these changes will significantly accelerate construction, particularly in the residential sector, which has seen a decline in single-family home starts due to material cost volatility.
articles.market_intelligence: Record-high copper prices and a roughly 50 percent jump in diesel prices since late February have contributed to soaring construction material costs. Single-family home construction fell across the country in the first quarter, while multifamily construction largely bucked that trend.

articles.title: Blackstone takes 54% loss on Seattle tower as it exits office game
articles.narrative: Blackstone has agreed to sell the U.S. Bank Center, a 44-story office tower in Seattle, to Spear Street Capital for approximately $280 million. This transaction represents a 54% loss for Blackstone, which acquired the property in 2019 for $612 million. The sale reflects the ongoing challenges in the Seattle office market, where demand has significantly declined post-pandemic due to increased remote work.

data_points.notable_features: 44-story office tower developed in the late 1980s.
data_points.project_notes: Blackstone previously acquired the building in 2019 for $612 million, with approximately $427.8 million in debt tied to the tower at that time. The 2019 acquisition also included the Docusign Tower at 999 Third Avenue in Seattle.

articles.market_intelligence: The Seattle office market saw $255 million in sales in the first four months of this year. In contrast, the Manhattan office market recorded $2.8 billion in sales, San Francisco had $1.5 billion, and Dallas saw $1 billion over the same period.

articles.title: NYC’s top deals: Nashville business exec snaps up Greenwich Village condo for $16.5M
articles.narrative: Northwell Health acquired a 360,000-square-foot former department store in Rego Park, Queens, for approximately $230 million from Alexander's, a real estate investment trust. Additionally, a mixed-use retail and office property in the Flatiron District, spanning 25,400 square feet, was sold for $31 million by an affiliate of L3 Capital to 893 Jobway LLC. The property is fully leased and stands five stories tall. In the West Village, a 20-unit apartment building totaling 11,400 square feet traded for $10.4 million.

articles.title: PolicyPro: Housing chief details NYC landlord court crackdown
articles.narrative: New York City's Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) is implementing a new strategy to expedite enforcement cases against negligent landlords in housing court. The initiative, led by Commissioner Dina Levy, aims to fast-track cases involving chronic neglect, properties under vacate orders, comprehensive litigation for outstanding violations, and Class C housing code violations. HPD is collaborating with the state Office of Court Administration to create expedited calendars for these cases, initially focusing on at least 10 housing portfolios with severe and persistent code violations. Additionally, a state bill targeting 'zombie homes' is close to passing, which would compel lenders of abandoned multifamily properties to address hazardous conditions, a move opposed by the banking industry.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Department of Housing Preservation and Development

articles.title: Ulta Beauty inks $400M Times Square lease at Jeff Sutton’s 1551 Broadway
articles.narrative: Ulta Beauty has signed a significant 15-year lease for all four floors of the 26,000-square-foot retail building at 1551 Broadway in Times Square, owned by Jeff Sutton's Wharton Properties. The lease, valued at $400 million, also includes the building's prominent 250-foot LED sign. Ulta Beauty plans to open an experiential store at this flagship location, replacing the former tenant.

data_points.notable_features: Includes a 250-foot LED sign; located in Times Square.
data_points.project_notes: Building was developed by Wharton Properties and SL Green in 2009 after demolishing the former Times Square Howard Johnson's hotel.

articles.market_intelligence: Times Square's retail recovery is uneven, with leasing activity concentrated in specific categories and apparel tenants largely absent. Experiential concepts are helping to fill space. Foot traffic in Times Square remains strong, with approximately 222,000 pedestrians recorded in November, an 18% increase from two years prior.

articles.title: A New York rent freeze is imminent. Bondholders still expect to be paid.
articles.narrative: A new analysis by Moody's Ratings estimates that a five-year rent freeze in New York City's stabilized apartments could lead to approximately 6% of multifamily loans defaulting by 2030. For buildings with at least one rent-stabilized unit, this figure rises to over 8%. The report highlights that while high rent growth in market-rate units might help some properties, many loans on rent-stabilized properties were already distressed, with only a portion of projected failures directly attributable to a rent freeze. The findings underscore the potential financial strain on landlords, particularly those with properties predominantly composed of rent-stabilized units, as the Rent Guidelines Board considers a rent freeze amidst high tenant rent burdens.

articles.title: Rudin makes splash outside of NYC with Stamford apartment buy
articles.narrative: Rudin, a family firm primarily known for its New York City holdings, is in contract to acquire 1 Harbor Point, a 239-unit multifamily complex in Stamford, Connecticut, from Building and Land Technology for approximately $150 million. This acquisition marks a significant expansion for Rudin outside its traditional market, with the property boasting 96% occupancy and proximity to public transit. The deal, if closed at the reported price, would value the property at over $627,000 per unit.

data_points.notable_features: The complex comprises two buildings and is within walking distance of the Metro-North train station, offering a commute to Grand Central Terminal in under an hour.

articles.market_intelligence: Annual rent growth in Stamford has been nearly 4% since 2021.

articles.title: South Florida’s top deals: Businessman nabs Palm Beach mansion for $43M
articles.narrative: A trust tied to Philip and Catherine Korsant sold a nearly 9,000-square-foot mansion in Palm Beach for $43.3 million to Thomas Swan, co-CEO and director of The Swan Group. The 1928-built property, situated on a 0.8-acre lot, features eight bedrooms, nine bathrooms, a gym, pool, and guest house. The transaction highlights the continued strength in the luxury residential market, with the property selling for significantly more than its 2017 purchase price.

data_points.address: 115 Via La Selva, Palm Beach, FL
data_points.notable_features: Eight bedrooms, nine bathrooms, three half-bathrooms, a gym, a pool and a guest house.
data_points.project_notes: Sellers purchased the property in 2017 for $14 million. The home was listed in November with an asking price of $49.5 million.

companies_people.label: SELLER
companies_people.firm_name: Trust tied to Philip and Catherine Korsant

articles.title: Flow affiliate sells unfinished Miami Worldcenter office, retail building for $27M
articles.narrative: An affiliate of Flow, led by Adam Neumann, sold an unfinished three-story retail and office building at Miami Worldcenter for $26.6 million in an all-cash transaction. The 60,000-square-foot property, which is 80 percent complete, was acquired by Daniel Cardenas and Michael Sullivan of Vertical Real Estate, along with Brazilian investor Gustavo Agostini. Merrimac Ventures will continue as a development partner on the project, which is expected to be completed this year, with the buyers actively seeking financing for the remaining construction and stabilization.

data_points.notable_features: Unfinished three-story retail and office building, 80 percent complete.
data_points.project_notes: The property is located within the Miami Worldcenter mixed-use mega-complex.

articles.title: Baptist Health sells Publix-anchored Cutler Bay development site for $30M
articles.narrative: Baptist Health South Florida sold a 25.5-acre development site in Cutler Bay for a total of $29.8 million in two separate transactions. The site is planned for a mixed-use project, including a Publix-anchored shopping center and a multifamily development. This sale aligns with Baptist Health's strategy to divest surplus land, capitalizing on developer interest in South Miami-Dade for residential and retail projects.

data_points.project_notes: The site was sold in two transactions: 15 acres for residential development and 10.5 acres for retail development.

articles.market_intelligence: Publix has been actively acquiring shopping centers it occupies across Florida, spending $326.5 million on such acquisitions over the past two years. Recent purchases include Hammocks Town Center in Kendall for $71.9 million and Fountains of Boynton near Boynton Beach for $78 million.

articles.title: Billionaire Adam Neumann supersizes Flow high-rise in Aventura
articles.narrative: Adam Neumann and his partner, Canada Global, received unanimous approval from Aventura city officials to expand their planned Flow-branded multifamily development at Aventura Corporate Center. The revised plans for the 8.6-acre site now call for 720 units across two 32-story towers, an increase from the previously approved 675 units in three 29-story buildings. The developers also committed $5 million to a city charter school and will provide community vehicles and bicycles in exchange for the conditional use approval.

data_points.notable_features: Two 32-story towers rising 367 feet; 20 units designated as 'hero housing' for city employees, first responders, and teachers; short-term rentals not allowed.
data_points.project_notes: Developers pledged $5 million toward a city-run charter school, along with 25 community vehicles and 50 bicycles for residents. The 8.6-acre site was previously acquired for $116.2 million in 2024. The existing 525,000 SF office complex will be retained, with one parking garage replaced and another expanded.

articles.title: Peruvian developer expands Miami condo pipeline with 62-story tower
articles.narrative: Peruvian developer Grupo T&C, led by William Ticona, is planning a 62-story mixed-use tower named 13th Edge near Miami's Brickell district. The project will feature 400 apartments and 400 hotel rooms, replacing a 36-unit condo building that Grupo T&C acquired last year for $27.6 million. This development marks a significant expansion of the developer's real estate activities in South Florida.

data_points.notable_features: Proposed 62-story tower with 234 parking spaces, a pool, bar, outdoor lounge for hotel guests, and a pool and rooftop yoga lawn for residents. Hotel rooms range from 400-900 SF, apartments from 400-860 SF.
data_points.project_notes: The site previously held El Vedado, a 36-unit condo built in 1972, which will be demolished. The developer acquired the site for $27.6 million last year. A pre-application has been filed with Miami-Dade County officials.

articles.title: Advocate Medical Group fuels Chicago medical office surge with pair of property buys
articles.narrative: Advocate Medical Group has acquired two properties in the Chicago metropolitan area, signaling a surge in the medical office market. One acquisition involved a 23-acre parcel in South Shore from Related Midwest for $15 million, where Advocate plans to develop a 40-bed hospital. The second purchase was an outpatient medical facility in Naperville, previously a Planet Fitness, which was redeveloped by Capital Healthcare Properties. These transactions highlight the robust demand for medical office assets in the region, driven by strategic developments and a growing need for healthcare services.

data_points.notable_features: The South Shore site is adjacent to Related Midwest's forthcoming $9 billion quantum computing campus and near the Obama Presidential Library.
data_points.project_notes: The Naperville property was redeveloped from a Planet Fitness into an outpatient medical facility, including an ambulatory surgery treatment center with one procedure room and cardiac services.

articles.title: Amli lists its apartment complex in Deerfield
articles.narrative: Amli Residential has listed its 240-unit mid-rise apartment complex, Amli Deerfield, for sale in Deerfield, IL. Built in 2015, the property is currently 99% occupied. The listing comes amidst a surge of apartment complexes hitting the market in the Chicago area, driven by strong rental demand and rising rents due to a constrained multifamily development pipeline.

data_points.notable_features: Located next to Interstate 94, across from a Hyatt Regency, and near major employers like Walgreens, Discover, and AbbVie.

articles.market_intelligence: The Chicago metropolitan area has seen strong rental demand and rapidly rising rent prices due to a diluted multifamily pipeline. In 2025, $4.5 billion was spent on multifamily properties across Chicago and its suburbs, marking the highest annual investment since 2022.

articles.title: How top South Side multifamily brokers became owners of $70M in real estate
articles.narrative: BSG, an investment firm formed by former Kiser Group brokers Noah Birk, Aaron Sklar, and Dan Gonzalez, has acquired a 16-unit multifamily property at 1552 North LaSalle Drive in Chicago for $6.2 million. The property, located in Old Town, recently underwent a gut renovation with condo-quality finishes. This acquisition adds to BSG's growing portfolio, which now totals approximately $70 million in Chicago-area real estate. The transaction highlights the firm's strategy of investing in stabilized, recently renovated buildings and boutique new construction, even as the broader South Side multifamily market, where the principals previously brokered many deals, faces increased financial distress.

data_points.notable_features: Recently gut-renovated with condo finishes.

articles.market_intelligence: Per-unit prices in Chicago's lower-cost multifamily neighborhoods surged to nearly $73,000 per door in 2024, up from less than $60,000 in 2019, according to inflation-adjusted DePaul University data. Cap rates in Chicago's South Side multifamily market previously reached double-digit figures, significantly higher than national averages of around or below 5 percent a few years ago.

articles.title: Once distressed office building in Rosemont lists following tenant boost
articles.narrative: A previously distressed, 255,486-square-foot office building in Rosemont, IL, is being marketed for sale by its special servicer following a significant increase in occupancy. The nine-story Columbia Centre III, located near O'Hare International Airport and major interstates, saw its occupancy rise to 75% with a weighted average lease term of approximately five years after a major tenant committed to relocating its headquarters to the property. The building was taken over by the lender in 2025 through a deed in lieu of foreclosure from its previous owner.

data_points.address: 9525 West Bryn Mawr Avenue, Rosemont, IL
data_points.notable_features: Located near Chicago O'Hare International Airport, the Donald Stephens Convention Center, and major interstates, with easy access to a mixed-use development and entertainment district.
data_points.project_notes: The building was seized by the lender in 2025 via a deed in lieu of foreclosure after the previous owner faced a $29 million foreclosure suit. The weighted average lease term is around 5 years.

articles.title: Pulte set to pay $14M to develop Naperville duplexes in unincorporated wetlands
articles.narrative: Pulte Home Company is poised to acquire a 32-acre undeveloped wetland property in Naperville, IL, from the DuPage Water Commission for $14 million. The developer plans to construct a 94-unit duplex neighborhood, named "Naperville Ridge," targeting empty nesters and pre-empty nesters. The project faces local opposition due to its wetland location bordering a forest preserve and concerns over tree removal, despite Pulte's adjustments to preserve some wetland areas.

data_points.notable_features: Wetland property bordering Greene Valley Forest Preserve.
data_points.project_notes: The site was appraised at $10.8 million. Two outlets will be designated as conservation areas, funded by the homeowners association. Pulte adjusted home types to aid wetland preservation.

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: Forest Preserve District of DuPage County

articles.title: Meet the Becks, the owner of the Dallas Mavericks arena site at Valley View
articles.narrative: This article profiles Beck Ventures, the long-time owner and developer of the Valley View Mall site in North Dallas, which is now the proposed location for the Dallas Mavericks' new arena. The firm, led by Jeff Beck and his sons Scott and Jarrod, acquired the 40-year-old mall in 2012 with ambitious plans for a $2 billion mixed-use redevelopment called Dallas Midtown. Despite securing $36 million in public tax incentives and holding a groundbreaking in 2016, the project has been plagued by delays, lawsuits, code violations, and a mysterious fire, with demolition of the old mall only recently completed. The Mavericks' plan to purchase 104 acres from Seritage Growth Properties for their arena and entertainment district will require a separate deal with Beck Ventures, which remains the largest landlord at the 110-acre site.

data_points.project_notes: The city of Dallas created a tax increment financing district in 2014, agreeing to provide $36 million in tax incentives, but pulled the deal when demolition did not start on time. Dallas voters earmarked $20 million for park land acquisition in the district two years ago. The site was declared a Habitual Criminal Property by the fire department in 2023 after three fires.

articles.title: Fort Worth Saddles up $10M incentive for first Panther Island high-rise
articles.narrative: Fort Worth is considering a $10 million incentive package for Seco Ventures' proposed 12-story mixed-use apartment high-rise on Panther Island. The incentive aims to bridge a financing gap due to lower projected rents in the undeveloped district and ensure the developer achieves a minimum 6.5 percent return on investment. City officials hope this project, which includes at least 290 apartment units and 9,000 square feet of ground-floor retail, will serve as a catalyst for further development on the man-made island.

data_points.notable_features: First major mixed-use development on Panther Island, a 338-acre man-made island surrounded by a 1.5-mile channel connecting the Trinity River.
data_points.project_notes: The city incentive would provide grants of up to $1 million annually for a decade, nullified if the developer earns more than 6.5% profit. The 25-acre site was previously acquired by Seco Ventures from Panther Acquisition Partners in 2024. The city received $443 million in federal funding in 2024 for flood control measures on the island.

articles.title: “We are in competition”: Eric Johnson decries City Hall gridlock after Mavs move
articles.narrative: Dallas Mayor Eric Johnson has publicly criticized the city's gridlock and its losing battle against suburban areas for residents, businesses, and attractions, following the Dallas Mavericks' decision to relocate their arena to the northern edge of the city. Johnson advocates for the redevelopment or sale of the aging Dallas City Hall, a site previously considered by the Mavericks, citing its extensive repair needs and the city's need to compete for future growth. This move by the Mavericks, along with other major businesses and sports teams, highlights a broader trend of companies migrating from Dallas' urban core to its northern suburbs, raising concerns about the city's economic competitiveness.

data_points.notable_features: Brutalist building designed by I.M. Pei.
data_points.project_notes: Estimated rehabilitation costs range from $152 million to $345 million by previous estimates.

articles.title: The Line, Hyatt Centric go back to lenders in foreclosure auction
articles.narrative: Two prominent Downtown Austin hotels, The Line Austin and the Hyatt Centric, were returned to their respective lenders in a foreclosure auction. JP Morgan reacquired The Line Austin after the previous owner defaulted on a $172 million loan. Separately, an entity affiliated with Peachtree Group took back the Hyatt Centric following a default on an $84.6 million loan. The Line Austin, a 428-key hotel, had opened in 2018 and was recently recognized as a top hotel in Texas, making its foreclosure particularly significant.

data_points.address: 111 East Cesar Chavez Street, Austin, TX
data_points.notable_features: Recently named one of the top hotels in the state by Condé Nast Traveler.
data_points.project_notes: The Line Austin was appraised for tax purposes at $168.9 million. The previous ownership defaulted on a $172 million loan issued in late 2023. The Hyatt Centric owner and developer Realberry defaulted on an $84.6 million loan from 2024.

companies_people.label: LENDER
companies_people.firm_name: PSOF LWC ASP Issuer V LLC

articles.title: Kalthia Group buys another San Jose hotel as Bay Area lodging market navigates defaults, foreclosures
articles.narrative: Kalthia Group Hotels has acquired the 88-room Super 8 by Wyndham in San Jose, CA, for $8 million. This purchase marks another addition to the firm's growing hotel portfolio in Silicon Valley, following two Sonesta ES Suites acquisitions in late 2025. The transaction occurs amidst a challenging Bay Area lodging market, characterized by increasing loan defaults and foreclosures, particularly among Super 8 properties and other hotels in San Jose.

data_points.address: 2050 North First Street, San Jose, CA
data_points.project_notes: This is the latest in a series of hotel acquisitions in Silicon Valley by Kalthia Group Hotels.

articles.market_intelligence: The Bay Area lodging market is experiencing loan defaults and foreclosures, with several Super 8 by Wyndham properties and other hotels in San Jose facing distress. New hotel construction in the region saw a 34.3% drop in rooms and a 28.6% decline in hotels year over year in 2025, with only 15 hotels totaling 1,610 rooms under construction.

articles.title: Investors buy another San Jose office building for nearly $14M
articles.narrative: A group of investors led by Silicon Valley executive Shri Chawla acquired an approximately 58,000-square-foot office building at 110 Baytech Drive in San Jose, CA, for $13.5 million. The property, currently occupied by green tech company Lyten, was sold by an affiliate of Money360, which had foreclosed on the asset in 2024. The purchase price represents a 6% discount from the building's foreclosure valuation.

data_points.project_notes: The seller, an affiliate of Money360, foreclosed on a $16.3 million loan for the property in 2024. The foreclosure proceeding valued the building at $14.4 million.

articles.title: Former Greyhound station in downtown San Jose slated to become concert venue
articles.narrative: A former Greyhound station and an adjacent vacant property in downtown San Jose are slated for redevelopment into an entertainment district named San Pedro South. The project, led by local entrepreneur Mauricio Mejia, will feature an outdoor concert venue called The Greyhound at the former bus depot site and another venue, The Domes, across the street. The initiative aims to activate these properties and create a walkable neighborhood while nearby multifamily developments await financing.

data_points.property_name: The Greyhound / The Domes (San Pedro South)
data_points.address: 60-70 South Almaden Avenue, San Jose, CA
data_points.notable_features: The Domes venue will be designed with inspiration from the Sphere in Las Vegas.
data_points.project_notes: The Greyhound site was seized by lenders out of foreclosure last fall after the former owner filed for bankruptcy. The project aims to activate the properties while adjacent multifamily developments secure financing.

articles.title: Westsider trails SF’s Kim for insurance commissioner, with ripples for resi market likely
articles.narrative: Preliminary election results indicate that former San Francisco Supervisor Jane Kim is likely to advance to the November runoff for California insurance commissioner, while real estate industry-backed candidate Patrick Wolff appears to be out of the running. Kim, who received nearly $30,000 from real estate industry players, campaigned on penalizing insurance carriers for delayed payments and advocating for single-payer statewide disaster insurance policies. The race has gained significant attention due to the 2025 fires in Los Angeles, which severely impacted California's insurance market, leading to concerns over the cost and availability of homeowners insurance.

articles.title: Housing champion vs. Pelosi’s pick for SF seat in Congress
articles.narrative: State Sen. Scott Wiener, a prominent advocate for increased housing construction, secured the top position in the primary election to represent San Francisco in Congress, advancing to a November runoff against Supervisor Connie Chan. Wiener's campaign, significantly supported by the real estate industry, champions the YIMBY (Yes In My Backyard) philosophy, asserting that housing shortages are the root cause of affordability crises. His platform includes a robust housing plan, proposing federal incentives of $10,000 per housing unit built by cities, funded by rolling back Republican tax cuts. Chan, while also a Democrat, offers a contrasting view on housing, expressing skepticism about the "all housing is good housing" argument favored by Wiener.

companies_people.label: EQUITY/FINANCING
companies_people.firm_name: National Association of Home Builders' PAC

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap Launches Network of Preferred Partners
articles.narrative: Marcus & Millichap has launched its Preferred Partner Program, an initiative to connect its commercial real estate clients with a curated network of third-party service providers. This program aims to support clients throughout the investment lifecycle by offering expertise in areas such as insurance, tax strategies, operational services, and risk management. The firm's CEO stated that the program extends their value-added approach, helping clients and investment professionals maximize returns by accessing valuable professional resources for informed decision-making and long-term investment success.

articles.title: Arrow Lines Up Acquisition Financing for 7-Property Mixed-Use Portfolio
articles.narrative: Arrow Real Estate Advisors secured a $69.7 million acquisition bridge loan for TARGO Capital Partners to acquire a seven-property mixed-use portfolio in Manhattan. The portfolio, comprising 127 residential units and 13 retail units across 76,182 square feet, is located in the Lower East Side, East Village, and West Village submarkets. The financing was provided by Bridge Investment Group, enabling TARGO Capital Partners to expand its investment platform.

articles.title: Avison Young Negotiates Relocation for Mercedes-Benz of Beverly Hills
articles.narrative: Avison Young has brokered a multi-decade lease for a 37,561-square-foot automotive dealership at 8825 Wilshire Blvd. in Beverly Hills, facilitating the relocation of a prominent luxury car brand. This transaction secures one of the last remaining automotive-zoned properties in the area with both sales and full-service bay capabilities, representing a strategic move for the tenant to expand its presence in the core of Beverly Hills.

data_points.notable_features: Last available automotive-zoned real estate in Beverly Hills with both sales and full-service bay capabilities.

people.name: Jodi V. Meade
people.title: Principal & National Director of Automotive Properties Group

articles.title: Ambrose Breaks Ground on 16-Acre Indianapolis Industrial Park
articles.narrative: Ambrose has commenced construction on Indianapolis Logistics Park – South, a two-building speculative industrial development spanning 16 acres in Greenwood, Indiana. The park will feature a 105,000-square-foot building and a 150,000-square-foot building, both designed with 32-foot clear heights to meet the growing demand for modern logistics, distribution, and manufacturing space in the Indianapolis market. The project is being financed through a construction loan from Pinnacle Bank.

data_points.notable_features: Two speculative buildings with 32-foot clear heights; Building I includes 2,900 SF office, Building II includes 3,200 SF office. Approximately 25 minutes from Indianapolis International Airport and the world's second-largest FedEx hub.

articles.title: BHI Provides Condo Inventory Loan for Ground-Up Luxury Project
articles.narrative: BHI, the U.S. branch of Bank Hapoalim, B.M., provided a $200 million condo inventory loan to Malabar Residences, a newly constructed ultra-luxury residential condominium and ground-floor retail building in New York City. The financing will retire the project's existing construction loan as it approaches completion and transitions into the temporary certificate of occupancy (TCO) phase. This loan underscores BHI's confidence in the New York City market and its commitment to supporting well-conceived developments.

data_points.address: 126 E. 57th St., New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Ultra-luxury residential condominium with ground-floor retail, units ranging from 508 SF to 2,992 SF.
data_points.project_notes: The loan retires an existing construction loan as the project nears completion and enters its TCO phase. Projected sale prices range from $1.36 million to $13.5 million per unit.

articles.title: Bond Market Turbulence Saps CRE Momentum
articles.narrative: The commercial real estate sector's recovery ambitions for 2026 are being challenged by bond market turbulence and elevated Treasury yields, despite a strong first quarter. Economists anticipate sustained high debt costs due to ongoing geopolitical conflicts, leading to a revision of earlier optimistic forecasts. While a new real estate cycle has begun, macroeconomic pressures are expected to keep cap rates flat, making it harder for investors to generate returns. Transaction volumes are likely to be smaller, with a shallower pool of bidders, emphasizing the importance of strong property management over relying on interest rate reductions. Office and retail sectors are identified as offering the best opportunities for returns, with office landlords increasingly willing to accept losses and retail benefiting from historically low new construction.

articles.title: U.S. Retail Supply Is Tightening, But Few Developers Plan To Build New Product
articles.narrative: The U.S. retail sector experienced negative absorption in Q1 2026, leading to high occupancy rates across major landlord portfolios. Despite this tightening supply, new retail development is largely stalled due to tariffs, elevated interest rates, and the specific pre-leasing requirements of retail tenants, making the financial risk unfeasible for most developers. Texas is an exception, with strong population growth driving significant grocery-anchored mixed-use developments and active expansion by major anchor retailers. Nationally, landlords are even buying out leases from bankrupt retailers to control spaces and secure higher rents from new, better-quality tenants.

people.name: Danny Finkle
people.title: Senior Managing Director and Co-Head of Miami Office

articles.market_intelligence: The U.S. retail sector experienced negative absorption of 4.4M SF in Q1 2026 after two quarters of increasing occupied space. Dallas-Fort Worth's grocery-anchored retail sector achieved a fourth consecutive year of record occupancy in January 2026.

articles.title: Bisnow Wins 10 NAREE Journalism Awards For 2025
articles.narrative: Bisnow received 10 awards at the National Association of Real Estate Editors Journalism Competition, matching its highest annual total. The newsroom earned silver for Best Real Estate Web Site, with individual reporters recognized across various categories. Notable wins included silver for Best Architecture Story for an exploration of commercial glass recycling, silver for Best Real Estate E-Newsletter for The D.C. Rundown, and an honorable mention for an investigation into the 2026 World Cup's potential impact on affordable housing. Editor-in-Chief Mark F. Bonner emphasized the importance of uncompromising journalism in commercial real estate, citing the consistent NAREE recognition as proof of the newsroom's commitment to cutting through spin and focusing on the story.

articles.title: SoCal City Decisively Passes First-In-Nation Data Center Ban
articles.narrative: Voters in Monterey Park, California, have overwhelmingly approved Measure NDC, enacting the nation's first permanent citywide ban on data centers. The decision follows growing resident opposition to data center development, including a specific proposal by HMC Capital for a 250,000-square-foot facility, which was withdrawn in April. This ban reflects a broader national trend of communities expressing concerns over the environmental and quality-of-life impacts of data centers, leading to moratoriums and proposed bans in several other states.

data_points.project_notes: Proposed data center project by HMC Capital was withdrawn in April 2026. City had a moratorium on new data center projects since late January 2026.
